Although transcriptomics studies have identified only few significant differences in gene expression patterns between conventional and GM maize plants fed at the same maturity stage [Bergström et al., 2018], future research using omics-based tools will provide additional information on how stacked traits impact posttranslational modifications, differential protein abundances or functional aspects of nutrients (e.g., bioavailability) after feeding corn silage to ruminants.

Furthermore, the use of transcriptomics approaches and further research on nonrumen feeding animals or cell cultures (e.g., hepatocytes) could identify differences in gene expression patterns between GM maize varieties that are fed to ruminants compared with conventional ones [Bergström et al., 2018]

However, transcriptomics studies using RNAseq technology have shown differences in gene expression patterns between conventional and genetically modified (GM) corn silage crops harvested at the same maturity stage when fed to non-rumen organisms such as fish [Tamashiro et al., 2016], dogs, cats or pigs that are maintained on a normal diet in terms of nutritional components and composition.
